title: National Register of Historic Places listings in Lac qui Parle County, Minnesota
rows: About 2 miles southwest of Montevideo off U.S. Route 212 44°55′58″N 95°44′54″W / 44.932861°N 95.748333°W / 44.932861 ; -95.748333 ( Camp Release State Monument )
Surrender site where prisoners taken by the Dakota people were released at the conclusion of the Dakota War of 1862 and the first military trials were held . Marked with an 1894 monument

Well-preserved example of a 19th-century Richardsonian Romanesque bank , built in 1892 for Christopher M. Anderson , an influential early pioneer and civic leader in Lac qui Parle County , and founder of Dawson

Multipurpose 1923 facility representative of the Minnesota National Guard armories of the 1920s , and the long-serving center of Dawson 's civic life , housing the city 's offices and principal meeting spaces

Neoclassical Carnegie library built 1917-8 , representing the philanthropy of the Carnegie Foundation , the work of regional architect A. H. Foss , and the civic importance of the Dawson Public Library , founded in the 1890s
Monumental 1899 courthouse designed by Buechner & Jacobson , and a symbol of Madison 's triumph in a protracted 17-year battle for county seat status
Lac qui Parle Mission Archeological Historic District
Address restricted 45°01′28″N 95°52′04″W / 45.024365°N 95.86789°W / 45.024365 ; -95.86789 ( Lac qui Parle Mission Archeological Historic District )
Sites of the 1826 Fort Renville trading post and an 1841 mission church ( reconstructed in the 1940s ) , plus associated Euro-American and Dakota habitations . Extends into Chippewa County
Lac qui Parle State Park WPA/Rustic Style Historic District
Off County Highway 33 at the southeastern end of Lac qui Parle 45°01′17″N 95°53′11″W / 45.021389°N 95.886389°W / 45.021389 ; -95.886389 ( Lac qui Parle State Park WPA/Rustic Style Historic District )

Three park facilities built 1938-41 , significant as examples of Depression-era federal work relief , the extensive New Deal projects conducted around Lac qui Parle , and National Park Service rustic architecture
One of west-central Minnesota 's best examples of a Victorian public school - built in 1911 - and an emblem of the efforts in the state 's small communities to provide local school facilities
One of west-central Minnesota 's earliest and most stylistically unusual Carnegie libraries , built 1905-6 , and one of three monumental public buildings forming Madison 's distinctive civic campus

Neoclassical city hall and opera house designed by Buechner & Orth and built 1902-3 , a long-serving center of civic and social life and a representative of Madison 's expansive early-20th-century community planning

description: This table lists entries from the National Register of Historic Places in Lac qui Parle County, Minnesota. It includes information about the name, date of listing, location, associated city or town, and a brief description of each historic place. The table serves as a reference for understanding the historical and cultural significance of these sites.
